sections.en_html:
• Etymology: The phrase fuses typology and synthesis. Typology joins Greek typos (τυπος, "impression, model, type") with -logia ("the study of"), hence "the systematic study of types" (Bailey, 1994). Synthesis from Greek syn- ("together") + thesis ("placing"), i.e. "a putting-together" into a unified whole (Kant, 1781).
• Literal meaning: As a compound applied here it denotes "the integrative classification of the kinds of ethical reasoning and ethically marginal tactics in negotiation" — a meta-level mapping rather than a first-order theory (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
• Arabic translation: The most precise rendering is توليف تصنيف أخلاقيات التفاوضTaṣnīftypology/classificationtawlīfsynthesistarkīb
• Historical names: In the literature it appears descriptively as "ethical frameworks/approaches in negotiation" (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020), the "four schools of ethical reasoning" (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998), and the "taxonomy of marginally ethical tactics" (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).

• Showcase: The ethics typology synthesis is the second-order discipline that organises the field's answers into coherent classification schemes — chiefly (a) the four ethical schools a negotiator may reason from, and (b) the empirical taxonomy of ethically marginal tactics (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998; Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• The four schools: (1) End-result / utilitarian (judge by consequences); (2) Duty / deontological (judge by rule and principle); (3) Social-contract (judge by accepted community/role norms); (4) Personalistic (judge by conscience and personal conviction) (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• The tactic taxonomy: Lewicki & Robinson's (1998) five empirically derived clusters: (i) traditional competitive bargaining, (ii) emotional manipulation, (iii) misrepresentation, (iv) inappropriate information gathering, (v) bluffing.
• Strict boundary (al-ḥadd): Its genus is meta-ethical classification; its differentia is that it does not itself rule on a single tactic but maps the dimensions and decision-rules by which any first-order ruling is generated.
(1) vs. Ethics in negotiation: the parent first-order field that asks "is this tactic permissible?"; the synthesis asks "by what classified frameworks do we decide?" (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
(2) vs. Normative ethical theory: general theories (Kant, Mill); the synthesis is their applied taxonomy specific to bargaining.
(3) vs. Negotiation styles (e.g. Thomas-Kilmann): a behavioural-disposition typology, not a moral-reasoning one.

• The exact phenomenon: The subject matter is the structure of classification itself — the dimensions, criteria, and decision-rules along which negotiation-ethics judgements and tactics can be sorted and inter-related (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
• Scope (1) — Reasoning frameworks: It studies how the four schools partition the moral question by their locus of evaluation: consequence (utilitarian), act-rule (deontological), agreed norm (social-contract), and conscience (personalistic) (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
(2) Tactic dimensions: it studies the empirical structure underlying the five tactic clusters — especially the cardinal split between tactics nearly all negotiators deem appropriate (traditional competitive bargaining) and those widely judged inappropriate (misrepresentation, inappropriate information gathering) (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
(3) Cross-mapping: it studies how each tactic is appraised differently depending on which school is applied — e.g. bluffing is licit under a game/utilitarian reading but suspect under a deontological one (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).

• Formalisation: The synthesis crystallised through two complementary lineages: the philosophical schools synthesised for negotiators by Reitz, Wall & Love (1998), and the empirical tactic taxonomy derived by Lewicki & Robinson (1998); both were consolidated into the standard pedagogy by Lewicki, Saunders & Barry (2020).
• Dated timeline:
(1) 1781–1863 — The source schools form: Kant's deontology (1785), Bentham's/Mill's utilitarianism (1789/1863).
(2) 1971 — Rawls' A Theory of Justice supplies the social-contract pillar.
(3) 1968–1995 — Carr (1968) and Strudler (1995) sharpen the tactic-level problematic (bluffing).
(4) 1998 — Lewicki & Robinson publish the empirical five-cluster taxonomy of marginally ethical tactics; Reitz, Wall & Love articulate the four-school framework.
(5) 2020 — Lewicki, Saunders & Barry integrate both into the canonical synthesis in Negotiation (8th ed.).

• Category: The synthesis is a meta-discipline — a second-order classificatory branch within negotiation ethics, kin to taxonomy in the empirical sciences and to meta-ethics in philosophy (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
• Subcategory: It comprises two limbs: (i) the reasoning-school typology (four schools) and (ii) the tactic taxonomy (five clusters), plus their cross-tabulation (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998; Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
• Its standing (nisbah): It is posterior to and dependent on ethics-in-negotiation for its raw material, yet architectonic over it, because it furnishes the ordering scheme that makes the first-order field teachable and comparable across cultures (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Neighbouring relations: It is parallel to meta-ethics (in philosophy), to nosology/taxonomy (in the sciences), and to the styles-typologies of conflict management (Thomas-Kilmann); it is the immediate child of "ethics in negotiation" and the sibling of cross-cultural negotiation ethics.

• Epistemological roots: The reasoning-school limb derives directly from the four canonical moral theories: utilitarianism (Bentham, 1789; Mill, 1863), deontology (Kant, 1785), social-contract theory (Rawls, 1971), and the personalistic/virtue strand (Aristotle, c. 350 BCE; MacIntyre, 1981) — transposed onto bargaining by Reitz, Wall & Love (1998).
• Methodological roots: The tactic limb derives from empirical social-science method — survey instruments and factor analysis on negotiators' perceived acceptability of tactics, yielding the five reliable clusters (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998); the underlying bluffing distinctions trace to analytic moral philosophy (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).
• Synthesising method: Its integrative move is cross-tabulation — appraising each tactic cluster under each ethical school — a method standard in applied-ethics decision matrices (Bowie, 1999; Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Islamic derivation: Where the Islamic ethic is engaged, the axioms again derive from primary text — the Qur'anic mandate of just measure (Q 83:1–3, against the defrauders, al-muṭaffifīnghishshnajsh

• Fundamental questions: The synthesis is organised around a logical sequence (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020; Lewicki & Robinson, 1998):
(1) Are the four schools exhaustive and mutually exclusive, or do real negotiators blend them? (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
(2) Is the tactic taxonomy descriptive or normative? Does empirical "perceived acceptability" settle moral permissibility, or merely report it? (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
(3) Why does "traditional competitive bargaining" rate as acceptable while "misrepresentation" does not — what dimension separates the clusters? (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998).
(4) How does each school appraise bluffing differently, and is the disagreement resolvable? (Carr, 1968; Strudler, 1995).
(5) Is the typology culturally invariant or do acceptability ratings shift across cultures? (Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Branches: (i) comparative-framework analysis, (ii) empirical tactic-perception research, (iii) cross-cultural typology validation, and (iv) integration with Islamic fiqh al-muʿāmalāt

• The fruit (al-thamarah): The synthesis yields a portable decision-map: a practitioner can locate any contemplated tactic within the five-cluster taxonomy, run it through the four schools, and predict both its moral status and its reputational cost before acting (Lewicki & Robinson, 1998; Lewicki, Saunders & Barry, 2020).
• Pedagogical value: It makes negotiation ethics teachable, examinable, and cross-culturally comparable, converting scattered intuitions into a structured curriculum — its principal scholarly utility (Reitz, Wall & Love, 1998).
• Intrinsic merit (al-faḍl): Its rank derives from the dignity of taṣnīfal-Muqaddimahʿadl

• Ruling on study (al-ḥukm): As an instrument serving just dealing, studying the ethics typology synthesis follows the ruling of its end: it is farḍ kifāyahfarḍ ʿaynal-Muwāfaqāt
• Threshold of fulfilment: The collective duty is discharged when the Ummah possesses enough specialists able to classify and appraise tactics so that transactions are reliably screened against ghishshghararnajsh
• Primary-text grounding: The screening criterion is fixed by primary text: "Woe to the defrauders" (Q 83:1) anchors the prohibition of deceptive measure; "O you who believe, fulfil contracts" (Q 5:1) anchors fidelity; and "whoever deceives is not of us" (Muslim) anchors the bar on misrepresentation. Thus any tactic the typology files under misrepresentationemotional manipulationḥarāmtraditional competitive bargaining
• Maqasid prioritisation: Under Maqasid al-Shari'ah, the typology must be deployed to prioritise ḥifẓ al-mālḍarar

• (1) Immanuel Kant, Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals (1785): The canonical source of the deontological school within the typology.
(2) John Stuart Mill, Utilitarianism (1863): The canonical statement of the end-result/utilitarian school.
(3) John Rawls, A Theory of Justice (1971): Supplies the social-contract pillar of the four-school framework.
(4) Roy J. Lewicki & Robert J. Robinson, "Ethical and Unethical Bargaining Tactics: An Empirical Study," Journal of Business Ethics (1998): The empirical five-cluster taxonomy of marginally ethical tactics.
(5) Roy J. Lewicki, David M. Saunders & Bruce Barry, Negotiation (8th ed., 2020): Integrates the four schools and the tactic taxonomy into the standard synthesis.
